coredata.dc:title: Carboxylated carbon quantum dot-induced binary metal–organic framework nanosheet synthesis to boost the electrocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Daqi
Guo, Huazhang
Huang, Kai
Zhang, Huiyi
Chen, Jia
Wang, Liang
Lian, Cheng
Wang, Yong
coredata.dc:description:
Two-dimensional (2D) metal–organic framework nanosheets (MOF NSs) are robust candidates for highly efficient oxygen evolution reactions (OERs). However, challenging top-down synthesis strategies still hinder electrocatalytic applications. Here, we directly fabricate 2D NiFe-MOF NSs induced by carboxylated carbon quantum dots (CQDs-COOH) in-situ through a one-step room-temperature “bottom-up” synthesis strategy without additional complex and low-yield exfoliation processes. Density functional theory calculations confirm that CQDs-COOH enlarges the layer spacing of the NiFe-MOF and effectively induces the formation of NiFe-MOF NSs via electron-withdrawing –COOH groups. Furthermore, CQDs-COOH increases the positive charge on the active sites and improves the OER performance of NiFe-MOF. NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH shows a low overpotential of 261 mV to reach a 10-mA cm−2 current density and a small Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1 and exhibits high durability after 48 h of long-term testing. These excellent electrocatalytic performances are also illustrated by application in a rechargeable Zn-air flow battery, which demonstrates an open-circuit voltage of 1.42 V, a 133.5° static contact angle, and an excellent specific capacity of 895.5 mAh
g
Z n
- 1
. The universal CQD-induced approach for bottom-up synthesis of high-efficiency 2D MOF NSs will facilitate the progress of 2D catalysts to construct state-of-the-art energy conversion and storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal–organic framework nanosheets
Carbon quantum dots
Induced synthesis
Oxygen evolution reaction
Zn-air flow battery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Zn-air flow battery assembly and measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We kindly thank Prof Honglai Liu for helpful discussions. The project was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21901154, 21671129, 22078088), Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis diagram and calculation analysis of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH. (a) Synthetic procedure for NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and their utilization for the OER. (b-d) the geometrical configuration and formation energy of NiFe-MOF (b), NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (c), and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (d), the yellow, gray, brown, red and write atoms represent the Fe atom, Ni atom, C atom, O atom, and H atom, respectively.

Morphological characterization of synthesized materials. (a–c) TEM images of NiFe-MOF (a), NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (b), and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (c). The inset in (b) shows the stacked few-layer nanosheets. (d–f) High-resolution TEM images of NiFe-MOF (d), NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (e), and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (f). (g–i) AFM images of NiFe-MOF (g), NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (h), and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (i).

DFT calculation and OER performance of synthesized materials. (a) The Hirshfeld charge at different metal sites of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH. (b) The Gibbs free energy profiles of intermediates and products on NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (Fe-site). (c) The difference charge density of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH. The yellow and blue isosurface denote the decrease and increase of electron density, respectively, and the value is ±0.003 e Bohr−3. (d, e) LSV plots (d) and the corresponding overpotentials (e). (f) Tafel plots. (g) Chronopotentiometry curves at 10 mA cm−2.

The possible enhancement mechanism of OER catalytic performance. CV curves in a potential range of 1.224 ∼ 1.324 V versus RHE of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (a), Cdl curves (b), EIS plots and the fitted equivalent circuit (c), high-resolution O 1s (d), Ni 2p XPS (e) and Fe 2p spectra (f) of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH. The red dashed box in (d) shows the amplified local M−O bond profile, which is illustrated by Fig. S22.

The performance of assembled Zn-air flow battery. Zn-air flow battery assembled with NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH||GO@CQDs-COOH, or RuO2||Pt/C (mass ratio is 1:1, catalyst loading: ∼5 mg cm−2). (a) Schematic of the zinc-air flow battery; (b) Open-circuit plots (inset: Light LED lamp with NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH||GO@CQDs-COOH as air cathode); (c) Air cathode contact angle and surface energy plots; (d) Power density versus current density profile; (e) Long-time discharge curves; (f) Charge/Discharge curves after cycling 168 h at 5 mA cm−2 current density; (g) Rate performance at 5–50 mA cm−2 current density; (h) Cycle stability plot of 2000 cycles at 5 mA cm−2 current density.

head.ce:title: Carboxylated carbon quantum dot-induced binary metal–organic framework nanosheet synthesis to boost the electrocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, and School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering
Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, and School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, and School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Two-dimensional (2D) metal–organic framework nanosheets (MOF NSs) are robust candidates for highly efficient oxygen evolution reactions (OERs). However, challenging top-down synthesis strategies still hinder electrocatalytic applications. Here, we directly fabricate 2D NiFe-MOF NSs induced by carboxylated carbon quantum dots (CQDs-COOH) in-situ through a one-step room-temperature “bottom-up” synthesis strategy without additional complex and low-yield exfoliation processes. Density functional theory calculations confirm that CQDs-COOH enlarges the layer spacing of the NiFe-MOF and effectively induces the formation of NiFe-MOF NSs via electron-withdrawing –COOH groups. Furthermore, CQDs-COOH increases the positive charge on the active sites and improves the OER performance of NiFe-MOF. NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH shows a low overpotential of 261 mV to reach a 10-mA cm−2 current density and a small Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1 and exhibits high durability after 48 h of long-term testing. These excellent electrocatalytic performances are also illustrated by application in a rechargeable Zn-air flow battery, which demonstrates an open-circuit voltage of 1.42 V, a 133.5° static contact angle, and an excellent specific capacity of 895.5 mAh
g
Z n
- 1
. The universal CQD-induced approach for bottom-up synthesis of high-efficiency 2D MOF NSs will facilitate the progress of 2D catalysts to construct state-of-the-art energy conversion and storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable electrochemical technologies of energy conversion and storage systems are promising candidates to address the challenges of increasing depletion of traditional fossil energy sources [1–3]. Among multiple secondary energy storage devices, Zn-air flow batteries (ZAFBs) stand out because of their safety, cleanliness, and high efficiency [4–8]. However, challenges of the overall efficiency and cycling stability of these high-performance devices, especially the sluggish kinetics of the electrocatalytic reduction and evolution of molecular oxygen, prevent their practical applications [9–13]. Precious metal catalysts (such as Ru, Ir, Pt, Pd, and their alloys) have been widely used in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalysis due to their fast kinetics in redox reactions [14,15]. Unfortunately, the absolute scarcity, high cost, and poor catalytic stability of these noble metal-based catalysts hinder continued commercialization progress.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted much attention in numerous electrocatalytic reactions (OER and ORR, etc) due to diverse molecular architectures and abundant active sites [16–19]. However, most MOFs do not show anticipatory catalytic performance because a large proportion of reaction sites are buried inside the framework, which reduces the reversible activity [20–22]. Compared to bulk components, atom-economical two-dimensional (2D) MOF nanosheets (NSs) obtain excellent electrocatalytic performance [23,24]. However, traditional “top-down” synthesis routes of 2D MOF NSs, such as wet chemistry and ball-milling methods have low yields and uncontrollable layers [25,26]. Recently, the bottom-up synthesis strategies to directly obtain 2D MOF NSs have been shown to realize the target of controllable layer number and large-scale synthesis [23,27,28]. The challenges of expensive ligands, complex steps, and special equipment restrict the widespread applications of current synthesis methods. Therefore, exploring advanced and high-efficiency preparation strategies for 2D MOF NS electrocatalysts is an essential pathway to handle these problems.

Low-cost carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are good candidates for in-situ assistant synthesis of 2D materials [29]. In addition, CQDs have attracted considerable attention in the catalysis fields due to abundant active sites [30–34]. Therefore, constructing composites that combine CQDs with other materials leads to robust reaction activity [35–42]. Overall, CQDs play bifunctional roles as structure regulators and active sites in the usage process, which can facilitate the formation of 2D materials and improve the electrocatalytic capacity of the complex. Herein, we have developed a one-step room-temperature in situ bottom-top synthesis strategy to synthesize 2D NiFe-MOF NSs induced by electron-withdrawing carboxylated CQDs (CQDs-COOH). According to TEM, AFM, XRD, Raman, and XPS analyses, the modulation of OER performance via morphology and structure was investigated. Thus, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH shows amazing OER performance and high durability. DFT calculations unravel the optimized geometric configuration and enhanced OER performance. Furthermore, composite CQDs-COOH and graphene oxide (GO) demonstrate intensive ORR performance. As a result, the composite is proven to be a promising low-cost and durable air cathode in rechargeable ZAFBs. This CQD-induced strategy opens a new method to develop 2D OER/ORR bifunctional electrocatalysts for highly efficient sustainable energy conversion and storage devices.

The NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs were prepared in CQD solution through a simple bottom-up process, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a (detailed synthesis methods see the Experimental Section). It is essential to note that the CQD-based synthesis strategy for 2D MOF NSs avoids the usual complicated high-temperature pyrolysis steps and efficiently preserves the intrinsically diverse architectures. To reveal the role of electron-withdrawing COOH groups in the synthetic processes, CQDs-OH with governing electron-donating OH groups was synthesized. As shown in Fig. S1a,b (Supporting Information (SI)), CQDs-COOH has a larger average size than CQDs-OH due to the more oxygen-containing functional groups on the shell of CQDs-COOH, which is clarified by their x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. The two CQDs have similar crystal plane spacings of 0.21 nm in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, which represent the (100) graphite plane (Fig. S1c, d). Moreover, CQDs-OH and CQDs-COOH have similar heights (Fig. S2), which reveals that different precursors can obviously modulate the horizontal size of CQDs and unadjust their height.

The interlayer spacing data of CQDs-OH and CQDs-COOH are consistent (3.83 Å and 3.82 Å), which indicates that oxygen functionalization does not modify their interlayer (Fig. S3). CQDs-COOH has smaller ID/IG than CQDs-OH in the Raman spectra (Fig. S4), which demonstrates that CQDs-COOH is endowed with a high graphitization degree. These two CQDs display the same stretching vibration bands in the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S5), such as OH at approximately 3145 to 3345 cm−1, CO at 1712 cm−1, C–H at 2825 to 3010 cm−1, CC at approximately 1592 to 1603 cm−1, and C–O at 1253 to 1177 cm−1. Although the bands of CQDs are similar to those in FT-IR, differences remain in O 1s in the XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. S6a,b and Table S1, there were only C and O elements to construct the CQDs in the XPS survey spectra. CQDs-COOH has a higher O content than CQDs-OH, which illustrates the nuance of precursors can lead to different contents of the consisted elements. The atom ratio trend of C-O (286.16 eV) decreases, whereas that of OC–O (288.56 eV) increases from CQDs-OH to CQDs-COOH in their C 1s spectra (Fig. S6c,d). Meanwhile, the atom ratio of the OC remarkably increases from 5.3 at% in CQDs-OH to 10.08 at% in CQDs-COOH in their O 1s spectra (Fig. S6e,f), which is consistent with the structure of the used precursors and C 1s spectra results.

To further study the composition and structural information of NiFe-MOF and its CQD derivatives, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectra (ICP-OES) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements of the as-synthesized products were implemented. As demonstrated by the XRD spectra in Fig. S7, only CQDs-COOH can strengthen the intensity of the characteristic peaks of NiFe-MOF, which are consistent with those of nickel hydroxide terephthalate hydrate (JCPDS No.035–1677) [23]. In addition, the as-prepared NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH has a similar crystal phase to the nickel-based MOF ([Ni3(OH)2-(1,4-BDC)2(H2O)4]·2H2O, CCDC No. 638866, triclinic crystal system), where Fe atoms replace approximately one-sixth of the Ni atoms (Ni/Fe = 5:1), according to the ICP-OES analysis results (Table S2). In addition, homologous Raman spectra provide more powerful evidence for structural evolution in Fig. S8. Consistent with the XRD results, ID/IG of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (0.71) and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (0.84) is higher than that of NiFe-MOF (0.58), which indicates that the size of the sp2 domain decreases when the CQDs are added. The speculation can be evidenced by the photographs of the macroscopic color of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (Fig. S9). The color of NiFe-MOF NSs after the addition of CQDs-OH changes from yellow to black whereas that after the addition of CQDs-COOH seems unchanged. The phenomenon further reveals that the NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH has a similar Raman construction with NiFe-MOF NSs and the Raman spectrum of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH closes to that of CQDs-OH.

To forecast the regulation capacity of CQDs-COOH/OH on NiFe-MOF, double-layer geometric configurations are established as shown in Fig. S10. After the DFT calculation, the optimized configuration of the designed materials significantly changes. The introduction of CQDs-COOH enlarges the layer spacing of NiFe-MOF NSs from 4.3 Å to 8.7 Å, while the layer spacing of NiFe-MOF NSs with CQDs-OH slightly changes (from 4.3 Å to 4.5 Å). It is confirmed that CQDs-COOH can prevent stacking between layers and effectively form 2D NiFe-MOF NSs. The formation energy (EF ) of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH further verify this phenomenon in Fig. 1b. NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (EF = 9.70 eV vs. NiFe-MOF) has a larger EF than NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (EF = −1.11 eV vs. NiFe-MOF), which illustrates that CQDs-COOH must absorb more energy to enlarge the layer spacing of NiFe-MOF. Overall, the DFT calculations reveal that CQDs-COOH can inhibit the stacking between NiFe-MOF layers and effectively construct 2D NiFe-MOF NSs.

The morphology of the resultant samples verifies the conclusions of theoretical calculations (Fig. 2 ). With the increase in added amount of CQDs-COOH, the MOF gradually converts from bulk, large sheets to the gathered small NSs in Fig. 2a,b and Fig. S11. Surprisingly, CQDs-COOH are uniformly attached to the surface of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (Fig. 2b), which have a similar size (4.45 nm) with the pure CQDs-COOH (4.42 nm) (Fig. S12). In contrast, a small part of CQDs-OH reunites next to NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (Fig. 2c), and the complex seriously aggregates with the increased usage amount of CQDs-OH (Fig. S13). High-resolution TEM imaging reveals that the interplanar distances of NiFe-MOF are ∼0.223 nm, which corresponds to the (410) crystallographic planes (Fig. 2d). Apart from the observed lattice of the primary MOF, lattice fringes of the introduced CQDs-COOH and CQDs-OH were found (Fig. 2e,f), which indicates that CQDs were successfully coupled to the MOF substrates. The corresponding elemental mapping images show that Ni, Fe, C, and O were uniformly distributed over the entire framework (Fig. S14). Importantly, the changes in their thickness were determined by AFM measurements. As presented in Fig. 2g-i, the average measured thicknesses of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH are approximately 60, 5, and 25 nm, respectively. The large thickness change demonstrates that CQDs play an essential role in regulating the thickness of the MOF. Therefore, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH exhibit a large surface area (283.5 and 162.8 m2 g−1), and the dominant pore size distributions are approximately 3.67 and 3.91 nm, respectively (Fig. S15a–c). Overall, the introduction of CQDs-COOH into NiFe-MOF can gain a uniform ultrathin and mesoporous structure of their composite, which endows them with significant oxygen and electrolyte transport capacity on the catalyst surface.

In addition to the morphology regulation of CQDs-COOH on NiFe-MOF, catalytic engineering was predicted and verified in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3a, Tables S4–S6, Figs. S16–S18, the active sites of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH were determined by Hirshfled charge. Since the OER reaction is an electron-loss reaction, the reactivity is higher at the site with a positive charge. The Hirshfled charge at the Fe sites (0.31–0.40) of the obtained materials is the highest compared to the Ni sites (0.20–0.25) and C sites (−0.04–0.19), which confirms that Fe is the active site of these three materials [23]. The Hirshfled charge at the Fe site of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (0.40) is higher than that at the Fe site of NiFe-MOF (0.33) and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (0.31), which indicates that the introduction of CQDs-COOH improves the OER performance. In addition, the free energy (G) of the obtained materials is calculated based on the Fe active site in Fig. 3b. According to ΔG of the rate-determining step, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (ΔG = 1.941 eV) has higher OER performance than NiFe-MOF (ΔG = 2.157 eV) and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (ΔG = 2.382 eV), which corresponds to the Hirshfled charge results. To further illustrate the OER effects of CQDs-COOH/OH on NiFe-MOF, the difference in charge density was determined in Fig. 3c. Due to the electron-withdrawing effect of –COOH, the positive charge (yellow) on the Fe site increases whereas the negative charge (blue) on the Fe site improves because of the electron-pushing effect of –OH, which demonstrates that the introduction of –COOH in NiFe-MOF can promote the OER reaction.

To confirm the correctness of the theoretical calculations, the OER activity was assessed by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with a three-electrode electrochemical cell in a 1.0-M KOH solution at an ambient condition. Notably, the catalytic activity of the composite was optimized by different types and additive amounts of CQDs (Fig. 3d, Fig. S19a,b). NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH exhibited the best OER performance with overpotentials of 261, 277, and 303 mV dec−1 at current densities of 10, 20, and 50 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 3e). In contrast, the NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH was inert to the OER. Furthermore, the Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1 of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH is always lower than those of NiFe-MOF (87 mV dec−1) and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (78 mV dec−1), which illustrates that the introduction of –COOH in NiFe-MOF can substantially promote the electrocatalytic reaction kinetics (Fig. 3f). NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH also demonstrated excellent stability in long-term electrolysis beyond 48 h (Fig. 3g). In contrast, the potential of RuO2 appears to remarkably increase in 10 h. Furthermore, the durability of NiFe-MOF and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH are also measured in Fig. S20, which is far away from that of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH. Meanwhile, the TEM images of these samples after cycling were tested, as shown in Fig. S21, it was found that the CQDs-COOH could still be uniformly attached to the NiFe-MOF NSs, which have a similar size (4.47 nm) with the CQDs-COOH (4.45 nm) before the electrochemical stability test (Fig. S22). Wholly, the morphology of the NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH is the same as before the electrochemical stability test, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH is one of the best reported NiFe-based OER electrocatalysts under similar test conditions (Table S7).

The CV curves of the NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH catalysts (Fig. S23) demonstrate that a couple of reversible redox peaks at about 1.34 and 1.53 V, 1.32 and 1.56 V, 1.38 and 1.55 V, respectively, which are consistent with the reduction and oxidation processes of nickel species between Ni2+ and Ni3+ [11,43,44]. And the electrochemical surface areas (ECSA) of the obtained samples were calculated via CV between 1.22 and 1.32 V vs. RHE to investigate the number of effective active sites (Fig. 4 a, Figs. S24-S25). The specific double-layer capacitance (Cdl) value of the as-synthesized NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH was 0.266 mF cm−2, which was maximal in all samples (Fig. 4b), and indicates the largest ECSA. To determine whether the outstanding OER activity of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH was due to the high intrinsic activity, we performed turnover frequency (TOF) calculations. First, the loading masses of Ni and Fe of the three catalysts were measured by ICP-AES, and are shown in the Table S3. The metal masses of Ni and Fe in NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH (98.1 μg cm−2) are slightly higher than those in other contrast samples. Then the TOF was calculated to compare the intrinsic activity of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH (Fig. S26), which was obtained according to previous literature [45,46]. By taking both Ni and Fe as the active materials regardless of the impact of the CQDs, the TOF value of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH was calculated to be 0.45 s−1 at 1.53 V vs. RHE, which was four times that of NiFe-MOF (0.11 s−1). This result further confirms the high intrinsic electrocatalytic activity of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH. Meanwhile, the EIS was recorded to better understand the OER reaction dynamics (Fig. 4c). From the enlarged picture (Fig. S27), Rs is approximately 5 Ω, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH has the smallest Rct of 15 Ω, which benefits the robust OER activity.

To reveal the effect of the structure of the sample on electrocatalytic activities, the elemental valence bonding states were characterized by XPS characterization. The signals from C, O, Ni, and Fe were detected in the XPS survey spectra (Fig. S28), which coincides with the ICP results and elemental mapping. Relative to the original NiFe-MOF (C-O at 286.1 eV and O–CO at 288.2 eV), the corresponding peaks of these samples slightly shifted to higher binding energies (C–O at 286.3 eV, O–CO at 288.4 eV for NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and C–O at 286.2 eV, O–CO at 288.3 eV for NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH) (Fig. S29). This phenomenon can be attributed to the addition of CQDs, which optimizes the electronic structure of the NiFe-MOF to form a more stable structure. The three main peaks in the high-resolution O 1s spectra originated from C–O (532.23 eV), M−O−R (531.18 eV), and M−O (529.48 eV). When CQDs were added, the M−O peaks of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH sharply weakened compared to NiFe-MOF, but they did not completely disappear (Fig. S30). This phenomenon implies that a portion of the M−O bonds converted into new M−O−R bonds, which can be confirmed from the enhanced M−O−R peak intensity (Fig. 4d). Meanwhile, the peaks corresponding to the M−O−R bond and C–O bond slightly shifted to higher binding energy due to the electronic effect of CQDs, which reduced the local electron density around the carbon atoms in the composite and resulted in higher bonding energy. The high-resolution Ni 2p spectra indicated that the binding energy peaks at 855.6 and 873.3 eV were attributed to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 signals [47], accompanied by two distinct satellite peaks (Fig. 4e). In the Fe 2p region (Fig. 4f), Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks were located at 713.2 and 725.1 eV, respectively, which are the characteristic binding energy of Fe3+ in FeOOH and important pristine active phase for the OER [48]. The main peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Fe 2p3/2 of NiFe-MOF are shifted to higher values after being coupled with CQDs, which resulted in a higher valence state for all Ni species and implies that Ni2+ is partly converted to Ni3+ ions. These XPS results show the electron absorption/donor effects, where new Ni/Fe–O–C bonds may emerge in the electrocatalyst and cause the electron transfer between C atoms from CQDs and Ni/Fe from the MOF [49]. The probable formation of new bonds that originate from CQDs should be an important ingredient to reduce the OER adsorption energy of the NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH. Optimizing electronic structures will facilitate the charge transport of the compound and promote the OER process.

To deeply understand the internal chemical composition of NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH samples, FT-IR was performed (Fig. S31a). Ni-O vibrations in the FT-IR spectra appeared at 450.5–485.4 cm−2 in Fig. S31b. The typical band at 511.1 cm−1 in the NiFe-MOF FT-IR spectrum belongs to the deformation mode of the hydroxyl group, but after recombination with CQDs, the O–H bond red shifted, which indicates that the addition of CQDs makes the hydroxyl group in the complex more stable [50]. The peaks at 756 and 825 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of aromatic and aliphatic C–H bonds, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1580 and 1383 cm−1 can be assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the coordinated carboxyl (COO–) groups. Similar to these O–H bonds, the asymmetric vibrations of the COO– groups in the complex containing CQDs also showed a redshift. These results are consistent with various characterization results above and further explain the electronic contribution effects of CQDs in the complex.

A homemade ZAFB was assembled to evaluate the practical application capacity, as shown in Fig. 5 a. To satisfy the bifunctional demand of the ZAFB cathode, the ORR electrocatalyst GO@CQD-COOH was designed and synthesized by a simple solvothermal carbonization strategy. GO@CQDs-COOH exhibits an onset potential of 1 V, a limited-diffusion current density of 3 mA/cm2, a half-wave potential of 0.7 V, and a large Cdl value of 1.81 mF cm−2 for the ORR under alkaline conditions, which is the best ORR performance among these samples in Figs. S32–S35. Moreover, a nearly 4e− ORR process of GO@CQDs-COOH is illustrated in Figs. S36 and S37. Noting, the catalytic stability of the GO@CQDs-COOH catalyst was determined by long-term 1000 CVs (Fig. S38), which makes GO@CQDs-COOH a candidate ORR electrocatalyst in the long-term operation of the ZAFB device.

NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and GO@CQDs-COOH were constructed as OER and ORR catalysts, respectively. Consequently, a physically equal amount of mixed NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH||GO@CQDs-COOH was used as an air cathode in the ZAFB. The open-circuit voltage (OCV) of the ZAFB with the synthesized material and commercial RuO2||Pt/C as air cathodes stabilized 1.42 and 1.43 V, respectively, which proves that the synthesized hybrid cathode has reached a commercial level, and the produced ZAFB can light an LED with 120 mW power density (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c, the air electrode with the loaded catalyst has a lower surface energy, and its static contact angle can reach 133.5°, which indicates its excellent hydrophobicity. The peak power density of the synthesized ZAFB was 61 mW cm−2, which is close to that of the RuO2||Pt/C-based ZAFB (78 mW cm−2) (Fig. 5d). In addition, the long-term discharged voltage (>1 V) at a current density of 5 mA cm−2 is shown in Fig. 5e. A larger specific capacity of 895.5 mAh
g
Z n
- 1
of the synthesized ZAFB is superior to the RuO2||Pt/C-based ZAFB (827.1 mAh
g
Z n
- 1
)
. Meanwhile, the synthesized ZAFB demonstrates outstanding charge/discharge profiles, robust current responses at different current densities, and near-perfect rechargeability after 2000 cycles (equivalent to 225 h) in Fig. 5f–h. Overall, the assembled ZAFB has excellent charge ability and stability and lays the foundation for replacing commercial noble-metal catalysts.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated a one-step bottom-up synthesis strategy to manipulate the high electrocatalytic activity of 2D NiFe-MOF NSs induced by CQDs-COOH. A facile room-temperature solution-phase method for the fast and large-scale synthesis of 2D MOFs without additional complex and low-yield exfoliation processes was developed. Employing Raman spectroscopy, XPS, and a series of electrochemical analyses, the offset valence state of the metal ions in the NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH electrocatalyst enhances the OER performance. NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH demonstrates excellent OER performance, delivers a low overpotential of 261 mV to reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1 with quick reaction kinetics, and exhibits superb electrocatalytic stability for at least 48 h without sharp activity decay. The electron-withdrawing –COOH groups in CQDs-COOH enlarge the layer spacing of the NiFe-MOF, effectively stimulate the building of the NiFe-MOF NSs, increase the positive charge on the active sites and improve the OER performance of NiFe-MOF NSs. These excellent electrocatalytic performances are further illustrated by application in a rechargeable Zn-air flow battery, which demonstrates an open-circuit voltage of 1.42 V, a 133.5° static contact angle, and an excellent specific capacity of 895.5 mAh
g
Z n
- 1
. Our finding may cause considerable reverberations to initiate brand new conceives of processing techniques for high-efficiency 2D MOF@CQD heterojunctions, and will stimulate further exploration of advanced MOF materials for sustainable conversion, storage devices and beyond.

O-dihydroxybenzene and pathalic acid were purchased from Adamas. Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), terephthalic acid (1,4-H2BDC), Trolamine (TEA), N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), ethanol, and potassium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide was purchased from XFNANO. 5% Nafion solution, RuO2 and commercial Pt/C were provided by Sigma Aldrich Corporation. Other chemicals and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification.

The CQDs were prepared by a previously reported one-pot solvothermal method [51]. Typically, o-dihydroxybenzene (0.250 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (50 mL) to form a homogeneous solution, and then the solution was transferred into a 100 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. When the reaction cooled down to room temperature, the crude product was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to take off the byproduct solids. Then, the solution is enriched using a rotary evaporator and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular retention weight: 3500 Da) for one week. Finally, the purified CQDs were dried at 80 °C for further measurements.

Similarly, the synthesis method of CQDs-OH, pathalic acid (0.250 g) was solved in 50 mL of ethanol and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave sustaining at 180 °C for 12 h. Then, the crude product was purified via the above-described pathway and obtained the CQDs-COOH.

In a typical synthesis of NiFe-MOF, 0.6 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (174 mg) and 0.18 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (44 mg) were mixed in 35 mL DI water, and magnetic stirred for 0.5 h (Solution A). Meanwhile, 0.3 mmol 1,4-H2BDC (49.8 mg) was mixed in 35 mL DMAC solution, and stirred for 0.5 h (Solution B). Then, solution A was transferred into the solution B under stirring for 15 min. Subsequently, 1.6 mL TEA was quickly injected into the solution. Afterwards, the beaker was continuously ultrasonicated for 8 hours under ambient conditions. Finally, the obtained product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol for three times, and dried in the oven for 12 h at 60 °C.

The series of products were synthesized by the same method as that for NiFe-MOF NSs except that CQDs-COOH was added into the solution B. And it was worth noting that when the amount of added CQDs-COOH solution were 2 mL (0.066 mmol), 4 mL (0.132 mmol), 8 mL (0.264 mmol), and 12 mL (0.396 mmol), the collected samples could be named as NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH-0.5, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH-2, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH-3, respectively.

The NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH was synthesized by the same method as that for NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH except that the CQDs-COOH was replacement by the CQDs-OH. And it was worth noting that when the amount of added CQDs-OH solution containing was 2 mL (0.056 mmol), 4 mL (0.112 mmol), 8 mL (0.224 mmol), and 12 mL (0.336 mmol), the collected samples could be named as NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH-0.5, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH-2, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH-3, respectively.

The 70 mg GO was first dispersed into a 70 mL ethanol under magnetic stirring for 30 min, and then was ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min. Subsequently, CQDs-COOH solution (4 mL, 0.132 mmol) was added under magnetic stirring for another 30 min. After the mixture solution was dispersed evenly, it was poured into 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 2 h. The resulting product was then dried in the oven for 48 h at 60 °C. Finally, the solid sample was placed in a tube furnace and carbonized at 800 °C for 2 h under a N2 atmosphere. The GO@CQDs-OH was synthesized by the same method as that for GO@CQDs-COOH except that the CQDs-COOH was replacement by the CQDs-OH.

Absorption spectra were tested by a Hitachi 3010 spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were registered on a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. TEM (JEOL, JSM-2010F; Talos F200X), High-resolution TEM (JEOL, JEM-2100F), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker) were used for morphology determination. The structural information of the synthesized samples was using an X-ray diffractor (XRD, BRUKER-D8A), the FTIR (Nicolet 8700, USA, 4000–400 cm−1 region). Micropore and chemisorption analyzer were measured by an analyzer ASAP 2020 M + C (Micrometritics Instrument Corp, N2). And the valence state of the element was performed on XPS (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, UK) with a mono anode Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) as the excitation source, while Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR, Horiba Yobin Yvon, excitation source: 30 mW He/Ne laser (633 nm)) were conducted to confirm the structure. The weight contents of metal elements in the synthesized composites were surveyed by an ICP optical emission spectrometer (Agilent 5110, RF Power: 1.20 KW). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a thermal analyzer (NETZSCH STA 409 PG/PC) under N2 condition.

The electrochemical tests were measured by a three-electrode system, which performed at an Autolab 302 N electrochemical workstation (Metrohm, Netherlands). In the electrochemical cell, a polished glassy carbon (GC) electrode (diameter = 5.0 mm and the surface area of 0.196 cm2) was used as the working electrode, while carbon rod electrode as the counter electrode, and a freshly prepared Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl electrode as reference electrode. And O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH and 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution were used as the OER and ORR electrolyte, respectively. The catalyst ink was fabricated according to the previous work [47], which contain 0.75 mL H2O, 0.25 mL isopropanol, 0.045 mL Nafion solution (5 wt% in a mixture of lower aliphatic alcohols and water, contains 45% water) and 5.0 mg catalyst. The obtained ink (10 µL) after constant ultrasonic treatment was uniformly loaded onto GC electrode, and were ready to test after drying under the environmental conditions. It is worth noting, all the potentials were calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the following equation:
(1)
E RHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.207 + 0.059pH

Prior to the OER measurement, bubble continuously in the solution with high-purity O2 gas for about 1 h, followed by continual CVs activation until stable CV was obtained. Then, the linear polarization curves under stirring at 1600 rpm were recorded, which performed from 0 to 0.8 V versus Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in a frequency range from 105 to 0.01 Hz and the potential was 0.48 V versus Ag/AgCl (perturbation: 10 mV). The chronopotentiometry was recorded under stirring at 1600 rpm (current density was fixed at 10 mA cm−2) to evaluate the long-term electrocatalytic performance. And the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) measurements were measured (potential range: 1.224 ∼ 1.324 V versus RHE, increasing scan rate: 20 mV s−1) to acquire the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA).

Before the ORR measurement, 0.1 M KOH electrolyte was saturated with O2 or N2. And O2 was still preserved over the electrolyte during the LSV tests. Then continual CVs activation were conducted until stable CV was obtained. Subsequently, the linear polarization curves under stirring at 1600 rpm were recorded, which performed from 0.1 to −0.8 V versus Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The 1000 CVs tests were performed to evaluate the long-term electrocatalytic performance.

The measurement of rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) was conducted with catalyst inks and electrodes prepared in the same way as the RDE measurements. The disk potential was conducted from 0.1 to −0.8 V versus Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. And the electron transfer number (n) was calculated by the followed equation:
(2)
n = 4 ×
I d
I d
+
I r
N

where Ir is ring current, Id is disk current, and N = 0.37 is represent the current collection efficiency of the Pt ring. The peroxide percentage (%H2O2) was calculated based on the followed equation:
(3)
H 2
O 2
% = 200 ×
I r
/ N
I d
+
I r
/ N

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zn-air flow battery assembly and measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous ZAFB was assembled according to the following process: above all, 6 M KOH, and 0.2 M Zn(Ac)2 were used as electrolyte. Then, a zinc foil which wiped with ethanol was used as the anode. Typically, the air cathode was fabricated by dropping catalyst ink onto the carbon paper. The catalyst ink was prepared by mixing 2.5 mg NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH and 2.5 mg GO@CQDs-COOH electrocatalysts with 0.75 mL H2O, 0.25 mL isopropanol, and 0.045 mL Nafion solution together, followed by constant ultrasonication until uniform ink was obtained. The current density for full battery test was normalized to the effective reaction surface area of the air–cathode, and the electrocatalyst loading on the air electrode is 1 mg cm−2. As a reference material, 2.5 mg 20 wt% Pt/C and 2.5 mg commercial RuO2 mixed electrocatalysts was prepared as the same procedure. Notable is, the full battery test was measured in air conditions without pure oxygen was pumped into the battery.

In this work, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) performance of the NiFe-MOF, NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH, and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH are considered. The reaction mechanisms for OER can be described as below:
(4)
H 2
O + ∗ → ∗ O H +
H +
+
e -
( Δ
G 1
)
(5)
∗ O H → ∗ O +
H +
+
e -
( Δ
G 2
)
(6)
∗ O +
H 2
O → ∗ O O H +
H +
+
e -
( Δ
G 3
)
(7)
∗ O O H → ∗ +
O 2
+
H +
+
e -
( Δ
G 4
)
where * represents the catalyst surface (Figs. S39–S41). For each reaction, the Gibbs free energy is given by:
(8)
G = E +
E
ZPE
+ ∫
C p
d T - T S
where E is the total energy calculated by DFT, EZPE is the zero-point energy, Cp is the heat capacity, T = 298.15 K is the temperature and S is the entropy. The EZPE , CP and S of intermediates are listed in Table S8. In addition, the Gibbs free energy of H+ + e− is half that of H2 [52]. The formation energy (EF ) of NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-OH and NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH has also been considered, the EF is given by:
(9)
E F
=
E
NiFe - M O F N S s @ C Q D s - C O O H / O H
-
E
NiFe - M O F
+ 8
μ H
- 8
μ
COOH / O H
where ENiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH/OH and ENiFe-MOF is the total energy of the NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH/OH and NiFe-MOF, μH and μCOOH/OH are the chemical potentials of the H atom and the –COOH/OH, respectively [53]. μH and μCOOH/OH are geometrically optimized energies of H atom, –OH and –COOH in the 15 Å × 15 Å × 15 Å boxes (Fig. S42).

The energy and the difference charge density are calculated from DFT by the Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with projector augmented ware (PAW) [54,55]. The exchange correlation functional is established by the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) method. The energy cutoff for plane-wave expansion is set to 500 eV. A 5 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack K-point grid was constructed by vaspkit. All the configurations are calculated using Gaussian smearing (SIGMA = 0.05 eV) and relaxed until the forces and the energy tolerances become less than 0.01 eV/Å and 10−6 eV/atom, respectively. In addition, the Hirshfeld charge is implemented in the CASTEP module of Materials Studio software [56]. All parameters are the same as the VASP.

According to previous literature, the geometric configuration of 6.3 Å × 10.2 Å × 34.5 Å NiFe-MOF is constructed [23]. NiFe-MOF NSs@CQDs-COOH/OH is constructed by connecting 8 –COOH/–OH on C atom of NiFe-MOF. To consider the influence of the layer spacing, the double-layer geometric configurations are established as shown in Fig. S10. A vacuum layer of 20 Å is established in the z direction to prevent periodic interaction.

Daqi Song: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Kai Huang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Huiyi Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Jia Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Cheng Lian: Supervision, Investigation. Yong Wang: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.
